schools.summary: Ellicott sets the flight against the events at Ramah; David's protest of innocence echoes the language of his Psalms.
Henry: Jonathan is the friend who loves at all times; David's trials prepare him for the kingdom he will reach through tribulation.
Gill fixes the moment: David fled while Saul lay bound in a trance at Naioth.
Keil and Ellicott read 'to him' as 'not' with the versions; Jonathan trusts that his father hides nothing from him.
Clarke and Wesley: Jonathan relied on Saul's oath of 1 Samuel 19:6 and expected to be told of any design.
Gill: the thing was too gross to be believed; any threat came only in Saul's frenzy.

themes: a son's reluctance to believe evil

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott: David reads Saul's heart more deeply than Jonathan; the oath (ki) is solemnly framed.
Clarke finds the 'step between me and death' figure in Juvenal; Wesley explains the confirming oath.
Gill: David swears to remove the doubt Jonathan still cherished.

themes: a step between me and death

schools.summary: Keil infers from David's plan a two-day civil new-moon feast, beyond the religious festival of Numbers 10 and 28.
Gill: Jonathan now credits David's fear and speaks to comfort him under his apprehension.
Clarke and Ellicott detail the lunar new-moon feast and its offerings; Keil reads the plan as turning on David's vacant seat.
Wesley clarifies that 'the third day' means the next day but one, David securing himself at Bethlehem or elsewhere.
Gill: the empty seat is a deliberate trial of Saul's disposition toward David.
Ellicott: the excuse was feigned and 'by no means sanctioned' by the historian; Keil: David did not regard prevarication as sin.
Henry: 'we have reason to think' the invitation was genuine, evidence of a religious family.
Wesley: Jonathan as royal deputy could lawfully grant such leave.

debates.question: Was David's Bethlehem excuse true, or a feigned pretext, and does Scripture sanction it?

sides.position: a feigned excuse, not approved by the sacred writer, showing David did not count prevarication a sin
a genuine invitation truly pleaded, from a religious family

schools.summary: Keil: 'evil determined' means firmly resolved; with Thenius he holds David was not yet certain of Saul's intent, against Seb. Schmidt.
Gill: Saul's calm acceptance would signal peace; his wrath would betray his design.

debates.question: Was David already certain of Saul's murderous intent, or did he genuinely need the test?

sides.position: certain to David though not to Jonathan
David was not quite clear as to Saul's intentions

schools.summary: Keil: 'a covenant of Jehovah' made with solemn invocation; Ellicott (with Ewald) on how the private scene was preserved, and on suffering as the road to greatness.
Clarke: David's fearless 'slay me thyself'; Wesley defines the covenant as sworn before God, who avenges its breach.
Gill: David asks to be told Saul's mind so he may appear at court or flee.
Keil takes the broken clause as an implied oath, not a question; Ellicott hears agitation in the incomplete Hebrew sentences.
Henry: Jonathan renews the covenant and entails it on his seed, fearing an Abimelech-like fate and looking ahead to Mephibosheth.
Gill: far be it from Jonathan to conceal any ill intention of his father.
Keil and Ellicott render the abrupt Hebrew and see David's fear that a suspicious Saul will cut off any messenger.
Clarke: the plain question is by what means the intelligence can safely be conveyed.
Gill: David asks who shall tell him whether Saul answered kindly.
Keil: the field is chosen to fix the sign unobserved and renew the covenant; Clarke: here Jonathan shows how word will reach David.
Gill: they withdraw so as not to be overheard by Saul's servants in the palace.
Henry lifts the friendship toward the surpassing love of Christ for sinners.
Keil takes the words as a solemn invocation introducing the oath; Ellicott surveys the versions and reckons the three days.
Clarke: something is wanting; LXX 'doth know', Syriac/Arabic 'is witness', Kennicott MSS 'liveth' — the last he prefers.
Gill (with Jarchi and Kimchi): the words are the form of an oath.

debates.question: What word is missing from 'O Lord God of Israel'?

sides.position: 'liveth' — 'as the Lord God of Israel liveth' (Kennicott MSS)
nothing supplied; the invocation itself carries the oath

themes: a textual gap in the oath

schools.summary: Keil reads the sworn promise to warn and send David away in peace, flowing into the covenant with David's house.
Clarke: 'as he hath been with my father' shows Jonathan knew God had appointed David to the kingdom.
Gill weighs the self-imprecation Jonathan lays on himself should he fail to warn David.
Ellicott: the broken Hebrew reflects deep emotion; with the versions he reads a plea that David spare his house even after triumph.
Clarke and Wesley: David is to show the covenant kindness of the Lord to Jonathan and his family, sparing them the fate of a displaced dynasty.
Gill: Jonathan does not doubt kindness for himself but pleads for his house.
Gill: the kindness is extended beyond David to Jonathan's family, and pledged for ever.
Ellicott: read the verse as the narrator's note, 'Jehovah required it at the hand of David's enemies,' not part of Jonathan's speech.
Wesley: the personal covenant is enlarged to the whole house of David, with an imprecation on any who break it.
Gill: the covenant is made with David's family, not with him alone.

debates.question: Is 'the Lord require it at the hand of David's enemies' Jonathan's oath or the narrator's comment?

sides.position: the narrator's own note that God avenged the covenant

schools.summary: Keil: Jonathan adjures David by his love, as in 1 Samuel 18:1; Ellicott: the prince is suppliant, dreading his orphans' fate under a new dynasty.
Wesley: Jonathan 'added' to make David swear, a restipulation that the covenant hold through all generations.
Gill: having sworn himself, Jonathan insists David swear to keep covenant with his house.
Keil defends the difficult Hebrew of 'third day' and 'go quickly down'; Ellicott voices Jonathan's resolve to let the empty seat read Saul's heart.
Gill anchors the day as the new moon, the first of the month.
Ellicott and Keil labour over 'm'od' (greatly, not quickly), 'the day of the deed,' and the unknown stone Ezel (perhaps the LXX 'argab').
Gill: David must stay three days away, not in the foodless field, but at Bethlehem or elsewhere unseen.
Wesley: 'when the business was in hand' recalls the earlier plotting of 1 Samuel 19:2; Ezel is a wayside marker stone.
Ellicott: a primitive, simple sign; Jonathan varies his plan, resolving to come himself rather than send a messenger.
Clarke details the archery ruse and the two watchwords, 'on this side' for safety, 'beyond thee' for flight.
Gill: three arrows for the three days of David's absence.
Keil: 'the arrows are on this side of thee' means peace; the sign is as simple as it is fitting, sealed 'as Jehovah liveth.'
Gill notes the lad is Jonathan's ordinary attendant, an unwitting instrument.
Wesley: the lad is sent out first, and the shot falls short or beyond as the news requires.
Keil: 'the arrows are beyond thee' means flee, for 'Jehovah sendeth thee away'; the covenant, sealed like Genesis 31:48, is an eternal secret.
Gill: arrows shot beyond the lad signal the worst to David.
Keil: 'the Lord is between me and thee for ever' makes God witness and judge, as in Genesis 31:48; 'the word' covers the whole renewed bond.
Gill: the matter spoken of is the covenant between the friends and their families.
Wesley: God is invoked as witness and judge should the league be violated.
Keil sets the feast and Saul's seat of honour by the wall, and reads 'Jonathan rose up' as yielding his place to Abner; Ellicott notes the LXX paraphrase.
Henry: Saul feasts on the peace offerings while plotting murder, an abominable sacrifice; David's absence is forced by peril.
Gill (with Wesley): David hid at the appointed time, having first gone to Bethlehem and returned.
Keil gives Saul's charitable 'he is not clean'; Ellicott (with Rashi and Abarbanel) reads 'Jonathan arose' as seating Abner in David's empty place.
Clarke: only four sat at the table, so any absence was quickly seen.
Gill: the king sat on his usual seat.

debates.question: What does 'and Jonathan arose' mean at the table?

sides.position: Jonathan rose and seated Abner in David's empty place so the king's side would not be conspicuously vacant
Jonathan rose from his seat beside Saul and gave the place up to Abner

schools.summary: Gill: Saul said nothing of David's absence on the first day.
Wesley: Saul supposed some accident had made David unclean and so unfit for the peace-offering feast (Leviticus 7:20).
Keil parses 'the second day' and notes the elder brother heading the family meal; Ellicott: two days' absence woke Saul's suspicion.
Wesley: 'son of Jesse' is spoken in scorn; a chance uncleanness lasted but one day.
Gill weighs whether a second feast-day was intercalary or simply for eating the peace-offering remainder.
Keil: Jonathan's answer corresponds exactly to the plan of verse 6; Saul's anger will follow at once.
Gill: Jonathan answers to excuse David, delivering the rehearsed message.
Ellicott: the LXX reads 'my brothers' for 'my brother'; the eldest brother was likely the convener of the district feast.
Clarke: the family sacrifice was a festal meal, the victim first offered to God, then eaten by the bidden.
Gill: a real Bethlehem peace-offering and yearly thanksgiving make the excuse plausible.
Ellicott: read 'son of perversity of rebellion,' an abstract Hebraism for a refractory man, not a slur on Jonathan's mother.
Clarke canvasses the versions and prefers 'son of an unjust rebellion' (a rebel against his father); Wesley: Saul taunts Jonathan's royal blood.
Gill: Saul's anger was kindled for Jonathan's giving David leave and excusing him.

debates.question: Does 'son of the perverse rebellious woman' insult Jonathan's mother or Jonathan himself?

sides.position: an abstract Hebraism, 'son of perversity of rebellion,' aimed at Jonathan as a refractory man
a literal reproach touching the mother, as the older versions took it

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'he shall surely die' and 'fetch him unto me' show premeditated murder at the feast; Keil: Saul hurls his javelin at Jonathan's question.
Gill: Saul reasons that Jonathan's throne is insecure while David lives, so he should surrender his friend to death.
Ellicott: Jonathan again tries to deprecate his father's anger, but Saul seizes his spear to strike his own son.
Gill: Jonathan bears the insults to himself in patience, but answers the sentence on David with 'What hath he done?'
Keil: the javelin-cast proves Saul's unalterable resolve; Jonathan rises grieved, certain his father means to slay David.
Gill: kings of that age bore spears as sceptres, so Saul's emblem of royalty becomes his murder weapon.
Wesley: Saul who professed care for Jonathan's throne now aims at his life — the madness of anger.
Ellicott holds both anger and grief, stirred by the public insult and by Saul's relentless enmity, the wrong to David wounding Jonathan most.
Clarke: read it rather of grief than anger — grieved for his father and for his friend.
Gill: Jonathan rose in fierce anger, resenting the spear and the resolve to slay David.

debates.question: Was Jonathan's rising chiefly anger or grief?

sides.position: chiefly grief — grieved for father and friend alike
fierce anger and grief together, the wrong to David wounding most

schools.summary: Henry: Jonathan faithfully keeps his promise and gives the signal; the sorrowful parting rests on the everlasting covenant, an emblem of gospel hope.
Ellicott: the meeting is briefly told, all having fallen out as David foresaw.
Gill fixes the time as the morning of the third day.
Keil and Ellicott: the single 'arrow' stands with indefinite generality for the three; the historian need not repeat that three were shot.
Gill: Jonathan told the lad to look for the arrows about the mark, the stone Ezel.
Keil: 'the arrow is beyond thee' is the signal; Jonathan hurries the boy so he will not see David, and the narrator notes the lad knew nothing.
Wesley: Jonathan called while the lad was still near, not yet at, the place of the arrow.
Gill fixes the spot the lad was told to seek the arrow.

themes: the doom spoken at a distance

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott: Jonathan sends the unwitting boy to town so he may take leave of David unobserved.
Clarke: 'make speed, haste, stay not,' though said to the lad, was also a warning to David that only prompt flight could save him.
Gill: seeing no one passing, Jonathan seized the chance for a few minutes alone with David.
Gill: the boy knew nothing of the meaning of the arrows or the haste; only Jonathan and David knew the matter.
Ellicott: the word means 'his implements'; render it 'arms,' not the modern 'artillery.'
Clarke notes this is the sole non-gunnery use of 'artillery'; the original means the bow, quiver, and arrows; Wesley agrees.
Gill: 'his vessels' or instruments — his arms, per the Targum.
Keil defends 'from the side of the south' as David's hiding place; Ellicott cites Josephus on the obeisance and reads 'exceeded' as David breaking down.
Clarke: David's grief was greatest, losing wife, country, and the altars of God, yet he waited on God's time — 'he that believeth shall not make haste.'
Gill: David came out as soon as the lad was gone, which he could see from his hiding place.
Keil: 'Go in peace' ends in an aposiopesis broken off by emotion, the oath standing 'for ever'; Ellicott quotes Phillipson on this noblest of friendships.
Gill: David goes in peace of mind, committing himself to God's providence and trusting the covenant to be kept.

themes: the Lord between their seed for ever
